pages.md:
Position Paper

# High-resolution CT phenotypes in pulmonary sarcoidosis: a multinational Delphi consensus study

Sujal R Desai, Nishanth Sivarasan, Kerri A Johannson, Peter M George, Daniel A Culver, Anand Devaraj, David A Lynch, David Milne, Elisabetta Renzoni, Hilario Nunes, Nicola Sverzellati, Paolo Spagnolo, Robert P Baughman, Ruchi Yadav, Sara Piciucchi, Simon L F Walsh, Vasileios Kouranos, Athol U Wells, and the Sarcoid Delphi Group*

One view of sarcoidosis is that the term covers many different diseases. However, no classification framework exists for the future exploration of pathogenetic pathways, genetic or trigger predilections, patterns of lung function impairment, or treatment separations, or for the development of diagnostic algorithms or relevant outcome measures. We aimed to establish agreement on high-resolution CT (HRCT) phenotypic separations in sarcoidosis to anchor future CT research through a multinational two-round Delphi consensus process. Delphi participants included members of the Fleischner Society and the World Association of Sarcoidosis and other Granulomatous Disorders, as well as members’ nominees. 146 individuals (98 chest physicians, 48 thoracic radiologists) from 28 countries took part, 144 of whom completed both Delphi rounds. After rating of 35 Delphi statements on a five-point Likert scale, consensus was achieved for 22 (63%) statements. There was 97% agreement on the existence of distinct HRCT phenotypes, with seven HRCT phenotypes that were categorised by participants as non-fibrotic or likely to be fibrotic. The international consensus reached in this Delphi exercise justifies the formulation of a CT classification as a basis for the possible definition of separate diseases. Further refinement of phenotypes with rapidly achievable CT studies is now needed to underpin the development of a formal classification of sarcoidosis.

# Introduction

Sarcoidosis is often viewed as a single disease of unknown cause that is characterised by the presence of non-caseating granulomas in multiple organs. However, a prevalent alternative view is that sarcoidosis is an umbrella term that describes a number of separate diseases. The rationale for the latter view includes major variability in patterns of lung function impairment (including obstructive, restrictive, and mixed ventilatory defects), natural history, treatment course, and putative microbial triggers. Biopsy data—the traditional means of distinguishing between entities in other interstitial lung diseases (ILDs)—have not been used to identify distinct pulmonary phenotypes in sarcoidosis.

Histological evaluation relies mainly on sampling of lymph nodes or small bronchoscopic specimens. The current study was undertaken to explore the concept that in pulmonary sarcoidosis, evaluation based on CT findings might provide a more discriminatory morphological classification as a framework for future clinical research and pathogenetic studies. The use of CT has several potential advantages. Although pattern recognition is common to both biopsy and CT, the latter allows evaluation of the whole lung parenchyma. In pulmonary sarcoidosis, it is well known that morphological manifestations are highly variable, to a degree that is rarely seen in other individual ILDs; however, in stark contrast to CT, the grouping or staging of sarcoidosis on chest radiography, as proposed by Scadding, does not allow fine morphological distinctions to be made. Against this background, to date, formal separation of morphological phenotypes based on CT appearances has not been attempted. The aim of the current study was to reach consensus on phenotypes in pulmonary sarcoidosis that are recognisable on high-resolution CT (HRCT) as a basis for the development of a morphological classification that will be applicable to future research centred on both pathogenetic pathways and clinical care.

# Methods

The study comprised the following two stages: development of statements, and a two-round online Delphi consensus process to rate the importance of the statements. The study was approved by the UK National Health Service Health Research Authority (Integrated Research Application System approval number 276717) and structured according to proposed methodological criteria for Delphi studies.

# Study groups and participants

For the purposes of the current study, a central Delphi Study Management Committee (DSMC) was formed, comprising four members: two radiologists (SRD and NSi), a chest physician experienced in the management of ILD (AUW), and a second chest physician with specific expertise in the conduct of Delphi studies (KAJ). In the first step, the DSMC invited a Core Expert Panel of 13 experts, from seven countries, to participate in the initial qualitative phase and 11 of 13 (85%) agreed to participate. Core Expert Panel members were chosen on the basis of the following: (1) an affiliation with and established practice in an international teaching institution; and (2) verifiable research contributions in sarcoidosis, ILDs, or both. The Core Expert Panel comprised chest physicians (n=5; DAC, ER, HN, PS, RPB) and thoracic radiologists (n=6; AD, DAL, DM, NSv, RY, and SP). The principal role of the Core Expert Panel was to participate in thematic interviews to facilitate the development of statements for the Delphi surveys.

# Position Paper

# Key messages

# Study design, management, and selection of Core Expert Panel members by the DSMC

- Despite the unifying presence of non-caseating granulomata, heterogeneity in lung function profiles, outcomes, treatment responses, and radiological appearances has led to the not unreasonable notion that sarcoidosis might simply be a convenient label for a group of different disorders.
- With CT, in contrast to biopsy material, morphological abnormalities in the whole lung can be evaluated; however, the use of CT to stratify patients according to morphological differences has never been attempted.
- As a prelude to the proposal of a new morphological classification of sarcoidosis, we sought to establish consensus on the principal or recognisable high-resolution CT (HRCT) phenotypes in sarcoidosis via a literature review and a two-round web-based Delphi process to rate 35 statements; 146 experts (pulmonary physicians and thoracic radiologists from 28 countries) were involved, with 144 participating in both Delphi rounds.

# Findings

- There was final consensus for 22 of 35 statements (63%) presented across the two Delphi rounds; participants reached agreement on seven HRCT phenotypes broadly termed non-fibrotic (comprising micronodular or macronodular patterns and consolidation) or likely to be fibrotic (manifest as bronchocentric reticulation with or without dense parenchymal opacification—with or without cavitation or fibrobullous destruction—or large bronchocentric masses, mimicking progressive massive fibrosis).
- Overwhelming agreement (95%) was also reached on a statement—not previously emphasised in published sarcoidosis guidelines—that HRCT should be performed at baseline in patients with sarcoidosis and evidence of pulmonary interstitial involvement.

# Future directions and implications

- This Delphi process sets the scene for future studies that should inform the development of a CT classification of sarcoidosis: first, variability between observers in the recognition of HRCT phenotypes must be tested; second, the prevalence of individual phenotypes in larger international cohorts needs to be established; third, the clinical and prognostic significance of patients not satisfying phenotypic criteria warrants evaluation; and, finally, research is needed to test whether (and, if so, how frequently) evolution from one morphological phenotype to another occurs in sarcoidosis.
- Final agreement on HRCT phenotypes, based on the investigations highlighted above, will not only support the development of a new morphological classification, but also anchor further avenues of exploration, including disease behaviour, genetic profiles, and important pathogenetic mechanisms in sarcoidosis.

- 1 new statement added

The DSMC, which comprised four experts (SRD, NSi, KAJ, and AUW), convened the Core Expert Panel, conducted preliminary themed interviews, and oversaw the Delphi process. The Nominating Panel (ie, members of the Fleischner Society and the World Association of Sarcoidosis and other Granulomatous Disorders) participated in the Delphi process and nominated up to three experts in the field (nominees). Delphi participants comprised Core Expert Panel members, Nominating Panel members, and nominees.

Correspondence to: Prof Sujal R Desai, Department of Radiology, Royal Brompton Hospital, London SW3 6NP, UK s.desai@rbht.nhs.uk

See Online for appendix.

Members of the Nominating Panel were invited both to participate in the Delphi process and to nominate up to three nominees (physicians or radiologists) with experience in sarcoidosis; the invitation letter to Nominating Panel members stressed the importance of achieving geographical and gender representation among nominees. The final list of Delphi participants comprised members of (1) the Core Expert Panel, (2) the Nominating Panel, and (3) the nominees (with the Nominating Panel and their nominees comprising the Sarcoid Delphi Group; see appendix pp 2–6).

pages.md:
# Position Paper

identify studies published between Jan 1, 1980, and Jan 31, 2021. Titles were screened by NSi for case reports and observational studies published in English that evaluated CT appearance in pulmonary sarcoidosis. Review articles were also used to identify any further relevant studies. The data gathered from the literature search were used to contextualise, but not influence, the subsequent interview stage.

# Delphi statements and surveys

To develop the statements for the Delphi surveys, a member of the DSMC (NSi) conducted individual virtual interviews on Zoom with Core Expert Panel members using open-ended questions designed to encourage experts to provide opinions on broad discussion themes. The interviews covered five broad clinical–radiological–pathological themes: (1) recognition of distinct HRCT appearances in sarcoidosis from the experts’ own clinical practice; (2) definition and categorisation of principal HRCT phenotypes; (3) clinical or prognostic implications of HRCT phenotypes (eg, response to treatment and survival); (4) possible relationships between HRCT phenotypes and lung function; and (5) potential relationships between HRCT phenotypes and bronchoalveolar lavage profiles. Interviews were continued until theme saturation was achieved (judged by the interviewer as recurring themes emerged).

Virtual meetings were recorded and transcribed using Rev, a commercial transcription service. Meeting transcripts were subsequently reviewed and condensed into key concepts by NSi using a content analysis approach. Statements for the first round of the Delphi process were created by the DSMC using concepts or themes raised during interviews with the Core Expert Panel and based on the clinical and radiological experience of the DSMC (appendix p 7).

# Statistical analysis

The definition of what constitutes consensus in a Delphi survey varies. For the current study, an a priori threshold of 70% or more agreement (strongly agree or agree) was used to define consensus agreement on the basis that a large number of participants were to be included, which would probably result in an inherently lower standard deviation in responses. Similarly, statements with 70% or more disagreement (strongly disagree or disagree) were considered to have met consensus for exclusion. Statements with 30% or less agreement in the first round were defined as “unlikely to reach consensus” and excluded from the second round. Therefore, all statements achieving agreement or disagreement of 70% or more and those with 30% or less agreement were not re-rated in the second round. Descriptive statistics were used to summarise overall scores. All free-text responses were provided in English, evaluated by the DSMC and, where applicable, used to make amendments or clarifications for Delphi statements in the second round.

# Results

# Core Expert Panel interviews

A total of 22 concepts or themes emerged from the interviews with Core Expert Panel members and centred broadly on the range of possible HRCT phenotypes, and the relationships between specific HRCT features and lung function tests, clinical features, and outcomes in sarcoidosis (appendix p 7). Ten concepts based on HRCT appearances were described in more than 90% of thematic interviews with Core Expert Panel members. Among these concepts, there was unanimous agreement that there are distinct HRCT phenotypes in sarcoidosis, but there were diverse views on what constitutes a distinct HRCT phenotype. For instance, although there was consensus that a peri-lymphatic nodular pattern.

represents a distinct HRCT phenotype, the suggestion of an HRCT pattern mimicking usual interstitial pneumonia was recognised by only a few Core Expert Panel members. After review of the concepts and themes by the DSMC, 34 statements were created—for example, the theme “progressive massive fibrosis (PMF) phenotype, even with airway distortion, can respond dramatically to treatment” gave rise to the following Delphi statement: “An HRCT pattern comprising large bronchocentric masses (ie, PMF lookalike) can regress with treatment”. See appendix (pp 8–9) for the 34 statements written in full, as presented to all study participants.

Demographics and characteristics of Delphi participants

Between March 23, 2021, and April 13, 2021, 146 of 174 individuals (84%; 48 thoracic radiologists, 98 chest physicians; mean duration in practice 21 years [SD 10 years]) who originally accepted the invitations to participate completed the first round of the Delphi process. The second round of the Delphi process took place between May 17, 2021, and June 17, 2021, and 144 of the original 146 participants (99%) completed the survey. See figure 1 for an overview of the study participants and consensus development process.

Position Paper










 Delphi statements reaching consensus agreement threshold (≥70% agreement)                   Delphi statements not reaching consensus agreement threshold (>30% to <70% agreement or <70% disagreement)
 Delphi statements meeting criteria for exclusion (≥70% disagreement or ≤30% agreement)

                                                                                                                                           Outcomes of Delphi surveys (%)

Delphi statement                                                                                                                           Round 1                       Round 2

1) HRCT should be performed at baseline in patients with sarcoidosis and evidence of pulmonary interstitial involvement                    Consensus agreement (95%)     NA

2) There are distinct HRCT phenotypes in sarcoidosis                                                                                       Consensus agreement (97%)     NA

3) HRCT features can be categorised as ﬁbrotic and non-ﬁbrotic                                                                             Consensus agreement (83%)     NA

4) Nodules alone or a predominance of nodules are almost always non-ﬁbrotic                                                                Consensus agreement (82%)     NA

5) A predominant ground-glass opaciﬁcation pattern is almost always non-ﬁbrotic (in the absence of ancillary HRCT features of ﬁbrosis)     No consensus (54%)            Consensus agreement (80%)

6) A predominant consolidation pattern is almost always non-ﬁbrotic                                                                        No consensus (34%)            No consensus (33%)

7) A predominant mosaic attenuation pattern is almost always non-ﬁbrotic                                                                   No consensus (42%)            No consensus (56%)

8) Nodules as the predominant or sole abnormality are a distinct HRCT pattern in sarcoidosis                                               Consensus agreement (82%)     NA

9) Multiple peri-bronchovascular, peri-ﬁssural, or subpleural micronodules are a distinct pattern                                          Consensus agreement (97%)     NA

10) Multiple randomly distributed micronodules are a distinct pattern                                                                      No consensus (57%)            No consensus (66%)

11) Multiple larger peri-bronchovascular nodules are a distinct pattern                                                                    Consensus agreement (78%)     NA

12) Scattered larger nodules are a distinct pattern (nodular phenotype)                                                                    No consensus (47%)            Consensus agreement (73%)

13) Scattered larger cavitating nodules are a distinct pattern                                                                             Exclude (18%)                 NA

14) Ground-glass opaciﬁcation as the predominant or sole abnormality is a distinct pattern                                                 No consensus (45%)            No consensus (60%)

15) Consolidation as the predominant or sole abnormality is a distinct pattern                                                             No consensus (62%)            Consensus agreement (92%)

16) Mosaic attenuation as the predominant or sole abnormality is a distinct pattern                                                        Exclude (29%)                 NA

17) Reticulation on HRCT almost always represents ﬁbrosis                                                                                  No consensus (59%)            Consensus agreement (80%)

18) Interlobular septal thickening on HRCT represents ﬁbrosis                                                                              Exclude (23%)                 NA

19) Large bronchocentric masses with or without cystic or bullous destruction almost always represent ﬁbrosis                              No consensus (62%)            Consensus agreement (83%)

20) Bronchocentric reticulation with or without dense parenchymal opaciﬁcation, without cavitation, is a distinct pattern                  Consensus agreement (81%)     NA

21) Bronchocentric reticulation and dense parenchymal opaciﬁcation, with cavitation, is a distinct pattern                                 Consensus agreement (78%)     NA

22) Large bronchocentric masses (ie, progressive massive ﬁbrosis lookalike) are a distinct pattern                                         Consensus agreement (79%)     NA

23) Predominant interlobular septal thickening is a distinct pattern                                                                       No consensus (43%)            No consensus (69%)

24) Mid-zone or lower zone-predominant subpleural reticulation with or without honeycombing (ie, usual interstitial pneumonia or           No consensus (39%)            No consensus (40%)
 non-speciﬁc interstitial pneumonia lookalike) is a distinct pattern

25) Pleuroparenchymal ﬁbroelastosis lookalike is a distinct pattern                                                                        No consensus (46%)            No consensus (69%)

26) “Soft” intra-thoracic (mediastinal or hilar) nodal calciﬁcation occurs in sarcoidosis                                                  Consensus agreement (96%)     NA

27) “Soft” intra-thoracic nodal calciﬁcation with hepatic and splenic calciﬁed granulomas favours histoplasmosis over sarcoidosis*         Exclude (45%)                 NA

28) Speciﬁc HRCT appearances correspond to speciﬁc lung function proﬁles                                                                   No consensus (60%)            Consensus agreement (80%)

29) A predominant reticular pattern on HRCT is associated with a restrictive lung function defect                                          Consensus agreement (71%)     NA

30) A bronchocentric pattern of ﬁbrosis is associated with an obstructive lung function defect                                             No consensus (54%)            Consensus agreement (72%)

31) A predominant nodular pattern on HRCT is associated with bronchoalveolar lavage lymphocytosis                                          No consensus (60%)            No consensus (69%)

32) Bronchocentric reticulation with or without dense parenchymal opaciﬁcation, with cavitation, predicts a poor response to treatment     Consensus agreement (82%)     NA

33) A minority of patients with sarcoidosis and a progressive massive ﬁbrosis lookalike pattern on HRCT can demonstrate partial or         No consensus (53%)            Consensus agreement (72%)
 complete regression with treatment

34) Lobar volume loss (particularly the upper lobes) is associated with a poor outcome                                                     No consensus (53%)            Consensus agreement (71%)

35) HRCT ﬁndings strongly indicative of ﬁbrotic disease are associated with bronchoalveolar lavage neutrophilia†                           NA                            No consensus (34%)

# Position Paper

Delphi statements were referring to the diagnostic utility achieved consensus agreement: (1) “HRCT features can be categorised as fibrotic and non-fibrotic” (83% agreement); (2) “Nodules alone or a predominance of nodules are almost always non-fibrotic” (82% agreement); and, after clarification or feedback in the second round (including the addition of the text, “In patients with a confirmed diagnosis of sarcoidosis”, which preceded all second-round statements), (3) “Reticulation on HRCT almost always represents fibrosis” (80% agreement); (4) “Large bronchocentric masses with or without cystic or bullous destruction almost always represent fibrosis” (83% agreement); and (5) “A predominant ground-glass opacification pattern is almost always non-fibrotic (in the absence of ancillary HRCT features of fibrosis)” (80% agreement). By contrast, the following Delphi statements did not reach consensus agreement: (1) “A predominant consolidation pattern is almost always non-fibrotic” (33% agreement); and (2) “A predominant mosaic attenuation pattern is almost always non-fibrotic” (56% agreement).

# Distinct (recognisable) HRCT phenotypes

On completion of the second round of the Delphi process, there was consensus agreement on seven HRCT phenotypes, which were broadly divided into the non-fibrotic and likely to be fibrotic subtypes (table 2, figures 3, 4). Of the seven agreed phenotypes, five reached consensus in the first round (consensus agreement 78–97%). Of the non-fibrotic HRCT phenotypes, the phenotypic pattern of “Multiple peri-bronchovascular, peri-fissural, or subpleural micronodules” had the highest level of expert agreement (97%). Only two phenotypes met the criteria for “unlikely to reach consensus”.

# Role of HRCT and consensus on fibrotic and non-fibrotic HRCT patterns

There was a high level of agreement among participants for the statement that “HRCT should be performed at baseline in patients with sarcoidosis and evidence of pulmonary interstitial involvement” (95% agreement) and that “There are distinct HRCT phenotypes in sarcoidosis” (97% agreement).

# Relationships between HRCT phenotypes and lung function or outcome

There was agreement on six statements that specific HRCT appearances correspond to specific lung function profiles, outcome measures, or responses to treatment: in particular, specific HRCT appearances were considered to correspond to specific lung function profiles by 80% of experts, with 71% agreeing that “A predominant reticular pattern on HRCT is associated with a restrictive lung function defect” and 72% agreeing that “A bronchocentric pattern of fibrosis is associated with an obstructive lung function defect”. In terms of relationship to outcome, most participants (72%) agreed that “A minority of patients with sarcoidosis and a PMF lookalike pattern on HRCT can demonstrate partial or complete regression with treatment”. Lobar volume loss was considered, by consensus (71%), to be a marker of poor outcome. There was also 82% agreement on the statement that “Bronchocentric reticulation with or without dense parenchymal opacification, with cavitation, predicts a poor response to treatment”.

| Multiple larger peri-bronchovascular nodules (figure 3B)\*                                                        | 78%                 |
| Scattered larger nodules (figure 3C)†                                                                             | 73%                 |
| Consolidation as the predominant or sole abnormality (figure 3D)†                                                 | 92%                 |
| Likely to be fibrotic subtypes                                                                                    | Consensus agreement |
| Bronchocentric reticulation with or without dense parenchymal opacification, without cavitation (figure 4A, 4B)\* | 81%                 |
| Bronchocentric reticulation and dense parenchymal opacification, with cavitation (figure 4C)\*                    | 78%                 |
| Large bronchocentric masses (ie, PMF lookalike; figure 4D)\*                                                      | 79%                 |

# Position Paper

# Figure 3

Sample HRCT images from four patients showing four phenotypes, considered to be non-fibrotic, that reached consensus in the Delphi process

- (A) HRCT image just below the carina showing innumerable peri-bronchovascular, peri-fissural, and subpleural micronodules.
- (B) HRCT image through the mid-zone of the chest showing multiple larger peri-bronchovascular nodules; some of the larger nodules have smaller surrounding micronodules (the galaxy sign).
- (C) HRCT image at the level of the carina showing scattered larger nodules (arrows), which appear to be unrelated to the bronchovascular structures.
- (D) HRCT section at the level of the aortic arch showing a pattern of predominant consolidation. HRCT=high-resolution CT.

# Figure 4

Sample HRCT images from four patients showing three phenotypes, considered likely to be fibrotic, that reached consensus in the Delphi process

- (A) HRCT image at the level of the carina with bilateral bronchocentric reticulation; there is also a pattern of loose reticulation in both lungs.
- (B) HRCT image through the upper lobes showing more extensive, symmetrical bronchocentric reticulation.
- (C) HRCT image just above the carina demonstrating upper lobe fibrocavitary disease; there is a mycetoma in one of the right upper lobe cavities (arrow).
- (D) HRCT image just below the carina showing dense, bilateral bronchocentric masses giving an appearance of progressive massive fibrosis; there are a few scattered micronodules in the left lung but this is not the dominant abnormality. HRCT=high-resolution CT.

# Discussion

The principal aim of this study—the first and largest of its type, we believe—was to evaluate consensus on recognisable HRCT phenotypes in sarcoidosis using a modified Delphi process. The study was undertaken as a prelude to the eventual formulation of a formal CT classification, as opposed to the historical approach in which a wide range of morphologies are lumped under the generic label of sarcoidosis. This historical approach runs counter to that used for other diffuse diseases in which splitting, variably based on pathological features, CT appearances, patterns of functional impairment, and disease behaviour or outcome, is the norm.

A CT phenotype classification has the potential to ground future research initiatives, providing a morphological framework for research and a substitute for histological evaluation. In principle, this might apply to further studies on the diagnostic accuracy of individual CT phenotypes, patterns of pulmonary function impairment, major separations in outcome, and relationships with genetic predilection and specific sarcoidosis triggers.

An important clinical message arising from the Delphi exercise was the strong agreement that HRCT should be performed at baseline in patients with evidence of pulmonary involvement. The strength of consensus (95%) was surprising: there is little doubt that CT is more sensitive than plain chest radiography but, to date, there has been no consensus or guidance on the role of HRCT in routine assessment. In the original 1999 American Thoracic Society–European Respiratory Society–World Association of Sarcoidosis and other Granulomatous Disorders Consensus Statement on Sarcoidosis, chest radiography was regarded as the mainstay of radiological diagnosis, with CT being reserved for the assessment of atypical or unclear findings.

The fact that a large majority of multinational experts in the current study supported the routine use of HRCT in evaluating the lungs at baseline is a potentially important consideration for future guideline development, although the important issues of cost, availability of (and accessibility to) HRCT worldwide, and the added radiation burden must not be ignored.

Participants agreed not only that there are recognisable HRCT phenotypes in sarcoidosis, but also that HRCT abnormalities can be categorised broadly as fibrotic or non-fibrotic. Among the HRCT phenotypic subtypes recognised by Delphi participants, there was widespread agreement about (non-fibrotic) nodular patterns and, in particular, the micronodular forms: in the first Delphi round, 97% of participants either agreed or strongly agreed that multiple bronchocentric, peri-fissural, or subpleural micronodules comprise a distinct HRCT pattern in sarcoidosis. This is not surprising because variants of a nodular pattern have long been regarded as classic signs on CT in sarcoidosis.

The distinction between nodular patterns, based on size, is interesting, with macronodules presumably reflecting coalescence of granulomata and supposedly signifying more extensive disease. Taken further, and in contrast to micronodular CT patterns, Aleksonienė and co-workers reported an association between the extent of macronodules on CT and lung function decline. The view that individual CT patterns...

pages.md:
# Position Paper

might be linked to particular patterns of functional attenuation pattern—reflecting obstructive small airways impairment is now supported by this international consensus study. Three other morphological phenotypes—namely, bronchocentric reticulation with or without dense parenchymal opacification but no cavitation, bronchocentric reticulation and dense parenchymal opacification with cavitation, and a PMF-like pattern—which, broadly speaking, we consider to be fibrotic, were identified through the Delphi process. In a previous small study comprising 80 consecutive patients with stage 4 disease on chest radiography, Abehsera and colleagues attempted to phenotype the different forms of fibrosis in sarcoidosis. In just under half of cases, there was predominant central bronchial distortion on CT; review of the CT images in the report suggests that this pattern corresponds to the pattern of bronchocentric reticulation without cavitation identified in our Delphi process. It is noteworthy that there was disagreement between the two observers in the study by Abehsera and colleagues in distinguishing between the bronchial distortion and linear patterns on one of the CT images.

A final, but important, critique of the earlier paper concerns the pattern of peripheral honeycombing in sarcoidosis. A pattern mimicking usual interstitial pneumonia has been reported in sarcoidosis. However, CT features not used in phenotype designation, especially traction bronchiectasis and distortion, need to be explored further. There was consensus agreement that in a minority of patients, a PMF-like pattern might partially or completely regress with treatment. Historically, this pattern of fibrosis has most often been linked to occupational exposures and, more often than not, signifies end-stage disease. However, on the basis of participant experience, a PMF-like appearance on HRCT might sometimes represent, at least in part, intense but reversible granulomatous aggregation.

A significant limitation of this study is that despite the multinational nature of the participant group, the higher representation of European and North American countries might have created bias, given that sarcoidosis manifestations might vary between countries and ethnicities. It is also important to emphasise that we do not consider the Delphi process to be synonymous with or a surrogate for high-quality research—the clinical and pathogenetic significance of phenotypic separation requires research that goes far beyond the scope of consensus agreement. However, research into the possible designation of separate diseases has, in the past, been grounded on histological distinctions that cannot be made effectively in pulmonary sarcoidosis. In this Position Paper, the Delphi consensus that HRCT phenotypic separations can be made is an essential first step towards a formal CT classification of pulmonary sarcoidosis.

Nevertheless, further CT studies, which we believe to be rapidly achievable, are required to inform the development of a formal classification. Observer variability needs to be evaluated, recognising that this might be.

pages.md:
Position Paper

# Panel: Proposed avenues for future research in pulmonary sarcoidosis

in this Position Paper supports the development of a CT classification framework for pulmonary sarcoidosis and provides justification for the use of such a classification for future studies of subgroup differences in genetic predilection, initiating pathways, mechanisms of progression, response to treatment, and patterns of lung function impairment, with implications for diagnostic algorithms and assessment of outcomes. In the absence of robust categorical histological separations between subgroups in pulmonary sarcoidosis, CT morphological distinctions could have a central role in future research.

# Considerations for studies in the formulation of a CT classification

- Observer variability in phenotype designation
- Prevalence of individual phenotypes (to include geographical variation)
- Further evaluation of patients not satisfying phenotypic criteria
- Possible evolution of phenotypes

# Exploration of CT phenotypes in the context of the following areas of investigation

- Robust diagnostic criteria
- Patterns of lung function impairment
- Disease behaviour (to include serial lung function change and mortality)
- Genetic profiles
- Triggers of sarcoidosis (to include combined genetic or trigger predilections)
- Key pathogenetic pathways

# Contributors

SRD, NSi, KAJ, and AUW formed the Delphi Study Management Committee. KAJ, DAC, AD, DAL, DM, HN, NSv, PS, RPB, SP, and SLFW formed the Core Expert Panel. SRD, NSi, and AUW were responsible for study conceptualisation, methods, investigation, study supervision, project administration, survey conduct, data curation, formal analysis, writing of the original draft, and reviewing and editing of the manuscript. KAJ was involved in the methods, survey conduct, formal analysis, and writing of the original draft. PMG, DAC, AD, DAL, DM, ER, HN, NSv, PS, RPB, RY, SP, SLFW, and VK were involved in the investigation and reviewing and editing of the manuscript. SRD, NSi, and AUW accessed and verified the underlying data reported in the manuscript. All authors had full access to all the data in the study and accept responsibility to submit for publication.

# Declaration of interests

SRD has received consulting fees for participation as an external expert steering committee member for a phase 3b/4 trial of trastuzumab deruxtecan in stage IV breast cancer from AstraZeneca and honoraria for travel expenses and for tutoring on radiology masterclasses in interstitial lung disease (ILD) from Boehringer Ingelheim; he is a co-founder and clinical director of, and holds shares in, DMC Radiology Reporting (an outsourcing, teleradiology company with no research or commercial interest in sarcoidosis). NSi has received honoraria for lectures from the World Association of Sarcoidosis and other Granulomatous Disorders (WASOG), Associazione Polo Italia-Cina, and Boehringer Ingelheim. KAJ has received grants from Three Lakes Foundation, the Chest Foundation, the University of Calgary Cumming School of Medicine, and the University Hospital Foundation (affiliated with the University of Alberta), consulting fees from Boehringer Ingelheim, Hoffman-La Roche, Pliant Therapeutics, Three Lakes Foundation, and Thyron SAB, and honoraria for lectures from Boehringer Ingelheim and Hoffman-La Roche; she has participated on the data safety monitoring board (DSMB) for the Ambulatory Oxygen for Treatment of Exertional Hypoxaemia in Pulmonary Fibrosis (PFOX) trial (no payment received). PMG has received grants from Boehringer Ingelheim, speaker’s fees from Boehringer Ingelheim, Roche, Teva, Cipla, and Brainomix, and travel support from Boehringer Ingelheim and Roche; he is a medical director of, and holds stock options in, Brainomix (a company that develops artificial intelligence-powered imaging technologies unrelated to the submitted work). DAC has received grants from Boehringer Ingelheim, the Ann Theodore Foundation, the Foundation for Sarcoidosis Research, and aTyr, consulting fees from Kinevant and CSL Behring, and travel support from Roche. AD has received consulting fees from Boehringer Ingelheim, Roche, Brainomix, and Vicore; he is a medical director of, and holds stock options in, Brainomix. DAL has received consulting fees from Calyx and has participated on the advisory board for Boehringer Ingelheim. ER has received grants from Boehringer Ingelheim, honoraria for lectures from Boehringer Ingelheim and Chiesi, and travel support from Boehringer Ingelheim; she has participated on advisory boards for Boehringer Ingelheim and Roche. PS has received grants from PPM Services, Boehringer Ingelheim, and Roche, consulting fees from Novartis, Behring, PPM Services, Pieris, and GlycoCore, honoraria for lectures from Chiesi, Boehringer Ingelheim, and Menarini, support for attending meetings from PPM Services and Boehringer Ingelheim, and fees for participation on a DSMB or advisory board from Galapagos, Pieris, Boehringer Ingelheim.
